$comment: SMGP-world engine dossiers (tagline + naming note + character paragraph + 3-paragraph history + quotes), keyed by canon engine id (palm-190-v10). DISPLAY-ONLY (never a fold input, exactly like team-profiles/driver-profiles/rival-quotes): shown on the SMGP dossier surfaces. Fully fictional SEGA universe. Canon (mission SMGP-024): the 17 permanent engine specifications from canon.json, one dossier per specification including the shared ones (LIZZIE 24 V8 serves TYRANT/BULLETS/COMET, VAPOR DNPQ V8 serves LOSEL/DARDAN, LORRY 32 V8 serves MAY/LINDEN/RIGEL/ZEROFORCE, RAM V12 serves LARES/MOON); a shared engine's dossier is the manufacturer's own story, with team-specific integration left to the car dossiers. VAPOR DN's architecture is deliberately unpublished in-world and is NEVER stated or implied here; VAPOR DN and VAPOR DNPQ V8 are distinct specifications. Names never change across the 17 seasons: annual development packages are recorded separately, never a rename, never a suffix. ASCII punctuation, no em dashes, authored to docs/content/SMGP_NARRATIVE_BIBLE.md voice.

palm-190-v10.name: PALM 190 V10
palm-190-v10.tagline: THE ENGINE THE ORDER OF THINGS RUNS ON.
palm-190-v10.naming: PALM is the Palm Court Works, a carriage-era engine atelier older than the circus itself, builder of engines for royal households before there was a grid to supply. 190 is the pattern number the works ledger gave its racing V10, and the ledger has never needed a new one.
palm-190-v10.character: The PALM 190 V10 is refinement as a weapon: a silken, wide powerband with no peak and no hole, a delivery so civilized it makes violence look common. It is modest in its thirst, over-engineered in its cooling, balanced to the gram and bolted together to outlast the men who bolt it. In qualifying trim and race trim it is the same engine, because the works considers a difference between the two an admission of fear. In the wet it is gentlemanly, under stress it is silent, and its maintenance burden is the lightest on the grid for the simple reason that it so rarely needs any. Its development ceiling was reached seasons ago; the atelier's work now is polish, and the polish shows.
history: The Palm Court Works began in the carriage age, building engines for people who did not ask the price and would not have tolerated the question, and it brought that certainty to the royal house of Madonna as its exclusive supplier. The engineering doctrine has never moved: an engine is not a component to be pushed but a standard to be upheld, and anything on it that can fail is a design error, not bad luck.

Its triumphs are the dynasty's own: six crowns and counting in the king's hands, whole seasons run without a raised voice from under the cowl, a preparation record so complete that a single cough in a decade ran on the front page above the results. Its famous failures are mostly theoretical, which is the point, though the Wet Season wrote one honest chapter: on the sodden afternoons when standing water levelled the machinery, the 190 could only wait, serene and dry-day sovereign, for the road to stop shining. When the Horsepower War opened and the paddock chased bigger and angrier, the atelier answered with refinement, and the refinement answered back on the stopwatch.

For seventeen seasons the works ledger has recorded one permanent specification, the PALM 190 V10, every annual development package filed against the same pattern number, never a rename, never a suffix, never a new architecture announced with a fanfare. The atelier's position is that a pattern which still wins does not need a new number; it needs, at most, a cleaner casting. The crown's engine keeps its name the way the crown keeps its throne: by never suggesting the question.
quotes: A Palm Court Works man, white gloves, unhurried: 'Other builders measure their season in horsepower gained. We measure ours in questions not asked. The 190 has not been asked a hard question in a decade.'

A rival engine engineer, over a beer: 'You want to hate it and you cannot. It is light, it is cool, it is frugal, it is silent, and it wins. The worst thing about the 190 is that it is exactly as good as they say it is.'

firenze-99-v12.name: FIRENZE 99 V12
firenze-99-v12.naming: The House of Firenze builds its own voice in its own engine works, and pattern 99 is the number the old maestro stamped on the first casting. The house calls the number a signature; everyone else calls it the finest sound in the paddock.
firenze-99-v12.character: The FIRENZE 99 V12 is an aria with a crankshaft: the sweetest, highest, most dramatic voice on the grid, revving far beyond where the sensible engines stop and making its best power in a narrow, dizzy band at the very top. It is thirsty, runs hot, is packaged with more art than margin, and is fragile exactly where it is fastest, the edge of the red line being both its home and its hazard. In qualifying trim it blazes; in race trim it wants nursing, a gloved hand on the mixture and a singer's sense of breath. In the wet it is brave and theatrical, and its maintenance burden is constant, loving and non-negotiable. The ceiling is artistry: the works will trade reliability for one more perfect note, and has.
history: The house engine works is as old as the house, and the V12 is its heritage made audible: twelve cylinders because eight would be common, tuned like an instrument because an engine, in this family, is a voice before it is a machine. The doctrine has never changed: power is a by-product of beauty, and a Firenze engine that does not make the grandstand rise has failed, whatever the stopwatch says.

Its triumphs are the pole-day arias, the tight-circuit masterclasses, and above all the one alpine winter Elssler carried the wine-dark song all the way to a title, the single crown the eternal second house is not supposed to steal. Its failures are sung about just as long: the hot afternoons it sang itself ragged and sagged at half-distance, the low-drag gambles where the 99 gave everything and the road ran out, and the perennial, private agony of being the finest voice in the paddock and the second engine on the big board. When the Horsepower War arrived the house did not change its note; it simply sang louder, and accepted the consequences.

Through seventeen seasons the works ledger has kept one permanent specification, the FIRENZE 99 V12, every annual development package filed against the maestro's original pattern number, never a rename, never a suffix. Aristocrats do not renumber their signatures. The casting is refined, the breathing is tuned, the red line creeps, and the name stays exactly where the old man stamped it.
quotes: A Firenze engine man, wiping rosin from his cuffs: 'You do not service the 99, you accompany it. Warm it gently, let it breathe, and on Saturday it will sing you a pole. On Sunday you must be brave enough to ask for the whole opera.'
A Madonna engineer, flatly: 'Beautiful engine. Lovely noise. We can always tell where the Firenze is on the circuit, we just follow the sound. It is usually behind us, and it is always loud.'

dick-md-v10.name: DICK MD V10
dick-md-v10.naming: Dick Motor Development was a two-car garage behind an airfield whose founder answered to Dick; the MD is the firm, not the man, a works designation the little shop stamped on everything it built. Millions bought the whole outfit and kept the stubborn little name on the cam covers, because the invoice said to.
dick-md-v10.character: The DICK MD V10 is a maverick's engine with a billionaire's spares truck behind it: powerful, peaky and hungry for revs, with a top end that arrives like a headline and a bottom end that is merely along for the ride. It is light for its output, packaged tidily, thirsty enough to make strategists sweat, and expensive to feed in every sense. In qualifying trim it is ferocious; in race trim it is managed, sometimes down to the last lap. In the wet it is loud but honest, and its maintenance burden is whatever the money says it is this week. Its development ceiling is the strangest on the grid: effectively none, because every time the little design reaches its limit, Millions simply purchases it a new one.
history: The MD series began in a one-room genius shop, the kind of operation with more talent than floor space, building an angry little V10 that embarrassed engines costing fifty times as much. Then the fortune arrived, bought the firm outright, and gave the genius the one thing genius always lacks: unlimited everything. The doctrine survived the purchase intact: rev hard, make power, let the money worry about the rest.

Its triumphs are loud: front rows invoiced and delivered, race wins taken at full roar, and one genuine crown, Alberti's, the season the whole gilded enterprise finally converted its spending into the one number the chequebook had never cleared. Its failures are louder: the afternoons it drank the tank dry at full volume, the certain results that ended in the barriers with the chrome still warm, and the recurring indignity of being out-scored by backstreet engines that cost less than its valve springs. The winter raids that stocked the Millions drawing office bought people, not peace, and the MD kept the whole paddock's respect anyway.

Across seventeen seasons the registered specification has stayed the DICK MD V10, one permanent name through every generation of the money, every annual development package filed against the same works designation. The firm is gone, absorbed into the empire, but the empire learned what the garage always knew: a name that makes power is worth keeping exactly as it is.
quotes: A Millions engine man: 'The MD does not know it was bought, and neither does the stopwatch. It revs, it shouts, it drinks, and on Saturday it is the angriest ten cylinders on the property. We just keep the spares truck close.'

A Bullets mechanic, wiping his hands: 'All that money and the thing still sounds like it wants a fight. Credit where it is due, pal: the engine is the only part of that operation with any manners at the hairpin.'

vapor-dn.name: VAPOR DN
vapor-dn.naming: VAPOR is the heavy-industrial combine whose engines power half the world's ships and generators; DN is the designation of its sealed racing specification. The combine publishes no architecture for the DN, not the layout, not the cylinder count, nothing, and the paddock has stopped asking.
vapor-dn.character: The VAPOR DN delivers shove rather than song: a wall of mid-range force that arrives early, stays late, and makes the long straights feel short. It is heavy, cold-blooded, efficient, and sealed at the factory, serviced only by the combine's own grey men, with the mechanics of the house it serves forbidden from opening it. Its cooling is industrial, its fuel appetite modest for its output, its reliability absolute, and it has exactly one mode, correct, in qualifying trim and race trim alike. In the wet it is traction-rich and unbothered. Its development ceiling is known only to the combine, which is, of course, the whole idea.
history: The DN arrived as the combine's engineering exercise and the gifted team's founding bequest: an engine given the way the seat was given, fully funded and expected to justify itself. The doctrine is the combine's own: an engine is a product, a product is a promise, and a promise is kept quietly. Whatever is inside the casings, the men who built it do not discuss it, and the men who race it do not need to.

Its triumphs came in a rush when the Horsepower War opened and the cathedrals of speed became the centre of the racing world: the DN was suddenly the class of the field down the long blasts, turning straight-line force into silverware with industrial regularity. Its failures are the seasons the arithmetic lost to artistry, the tight, twisting rounds where force was the wrong currency, and the long, public mystery of the technical pages, which have filled whole columns with the question of what the DN actually is and never once printed an answer. The combine regards the speculation as free advertising.

For seventeen seasons the registered specification has been the VAPOR DN, one permanent, unpublished, unopened designation, every annual development package sealed and filed under the same letters. It is a distinct specification from the customer DNPQ V8 the combine sells elsewhere, and the combine has never once blurred the two. A product that keeps its promises does not need a new name; it needs only to keep them.
quotes: A VAPOR grey man, closing the casings: 'There is nothing for your mechanics inside, gentlemen. The DN is maintained, it is not opened. You will find the results in the timing screens, which is where the combine prefers to publish.'

A rival engineer, arms folded at the fence: 'Seventeen seasons and nobody knows what it is. Down a straight, it is the most correct violence on the calendar, that is what it is. The rest is a locked box and a smile.'

delta-103-v10.name: DELTA 103 V10
delta-103-v10.naming: Delta Instrument and Gauge built the paddock's gauges before it built an engine, and it numbers its engines like instruments. 103 is the hundred-and-third pattern off the drawing office, recorded in the same tidy hand as the hundred-and-two before it.
delta-103-v10.character: The DELTA 103 V10 is the most predictable engine on the grid: linear, smooth and honest from the first rev to the last, with a modest peak it never pretends is anything else. It is frugal, cool-running, kind to its own internals and so consistent that a driver can brake by the tachometer. In qualifying trim it is honest; in race trim it is identical, because Delta considers a trim difference a measurement error. In the wet it is calm, its maintenance burden is scheduled to the minute, and its reliability is metronomic. Its development ceiling is reached by increments, one logged improvement at a time, and Delta has never once apologized for the size of the increments.
history: Delta came to engines the way it came to gauges: by measurement. The drawing office treated the racing V10 as an instrument that happened to produce power, and the doctrine has never wavered, that an engine you cannot predict is an engine you cannot trust, and a race is long. The white-glove house of Blanche is not a customer so much as a control experiment that has been running for seventeen seasons.

Its triumphs are the near things done perfectly: the long, spotless LEVEL B campaigns powered without a single mechanical stain, sixteen flawless rounds in a row logged and filed, podiums collected by consistency while brasher engines broke. Its failures are the mirror image, the big days the peak number fell one tenth short of promotion, the wet gambles the 103 was too honest to win, the whole paddock knowing exactly what the Delta would do on Sunday, and the Delta doing it, one place short. Delta files those afternoons too, under limitations, and returns a fraction sharper.

Through seventeen seasons the drawing office has kept one permanent specification, the DELTA 103 V10, every annual development package logged against the same pattern number, never a rename, never a suffix. An instrument does not change its designation because the weather did. The hundred-and-third pattern endures, and the gauge, as ever, does not lie about its own name.
quotes: A Delta works man, ledger in hand: 'The 103 will produce exactly this power, at exactly this temperature, for exactly this distance. If that is not enough to win your race, the deficiency is not in the engine, and I say that with the numbers to prove it.'

A Blanche whitecoat: 'People ask why we keep it. Because on Sunday at five o'clock, the 103 has never once lied to us. You cannot say that about anything else in this paddock, including the weather men.'

lizzie-24-v8.name: LIZZIE 24 V8
lizzie-24-v8.tagline: THE PEOPLE'S ENGINE, SMALL AND FEARLESS.
lizzie-24-v8.naming: Lizzie Engineering is a stubborn family firm named for the founder's daughter, who still signs the castings herself. 24 was her age the summer the first V8 ran, and the family has never once been persuaded to change a number that personal.
lizzie-24-v8.character: The LIZZIE 24 V8 is a little engine with a fighter's heart: light, punchy and eager to rev, with a sweet, narrow band it will hold all afternoon if you let it sing. It is simple, cheap to keep, modest in its thirst and its cooling, and honest in the wet, where its crisp response makes a brave man braver. It is also fragile if over-stressed, and every one of its three customer houses leans on it differently: Tyrant leans late and hard, Bullets leans to the red line of sense, Comet leans until something lets go. In qualifying trim it is eager; in race trim it depends entirely on the garage's mercy. Its ceiling is lower than the works engines', and its people have made a whole philosophy of not caring.
history: Lizzie Engineering began as a family shop with a daughter's name over the door and a conviction that a small, brave engine, properly loved, could trouble engines built by empires. It sells to the houses the empires ignore, and it has never wanted the empires' business. The doctrine is the founder's own: keep it light, keep it simple, keep it revving, and never sell a customer more engine than he can hold together.

Its triumphs are the insurgency's own: Ceara's six career wins from a backstreet garage, the Wet Season afternoons when the little V8 paddled LEVEL A machinery through the standing water of the forest esses, Tyrant's brake-late raids on the street rounds, and Comet's one blazing summer when the 24 briefly carried a tuning shop past the aristocracy. Its failures are written in smoke: the over-stressed units that let go with results in sight, Comet's fortnightly rebuilds, the qualifying heroics that had no race trim behind them. The family signs every casting anyway; a Lizzie that fails flat out has, by the firm's own creed, died correctly.

Across seventeen seasons the registered specification has stayed the LIZZIE 24 V8, one permanent name serving three houses, every annual development package filed against the same pattern, never a rename, never a suffix. The daughter still signs the castings. An engine named for a person does not get renamed for a season; it gets another year older, and another year braver.
quotes: A Lizzie family man, casting pen in hand: 'We do not make the biggest engine and we do not make the cleverest one. We make the 24, and we make it for people who race like they mean it. The name on the casing is my daughter's. That is the whole warranty.'

A Tyrant chief mechanic: 'The little Lizzie will hold the red line all afternoon if you build around it honestly. Abuse it and it dies, sure. Everything dies. The 24 dies brave, which is more than I can say for some engines costing ten times as much.'

vapor-dnpq-v8.name: VAPOR DNPQ V8
vapor-dnpq-v8.naming: The DNPQ is VAPOR's published-architecture V8 for customer houses, a distinct specification from the sealed DN the combine reserves for Bestowal. The letters are a works designation, not a version, and in seventeen seasons the name has never been incremented.
vapor-dnpq-v8.character: The VAPOR DNPQ V8 is the DN's plain-spoken cousin: torquier and coarser, a touch down on ultimate power, and free of mystery. Its delivery is steady and dependable, its cooling friendly, its thirst moderate, and its documentation complete, a customer engine a customer's own mechanics may actually open. It is robust, durable and honest in race trim, solid rather than electric in qualifying, and dependable in the wet, where its unhurried shove finds grip. Its maintenance burden is light and its temperament lighter. Its development ceiling is set by the combine's kindness, and both its houses, Losel's calculators and Dardan's forge, have learned to extract everything the kindness allows.
history: The DNPQ exists because the combine sells what it does not seal: a customer V8 with published drawings, honest tolerances and no grey men. The doctrine is industrial courtesy: a customer house deserves an engine it can understand, maintain and trust, and it deserves to know exactly what it is not getting, which is the DN. Two very different houses have made the DNPQ their own, Losel reading it like a data sheet, Dardan swinging it like a hammer.

Its triumphs are the honest campaigns of the midfield's proudest teams: Losel's whisker-close LEVEL B seasons, whole metronomic stints where the customer V8 outlasted works machinery, and Dardan's street-round ambushes, the stout shove out of slow corners putting bronze paint on aristocratic flanks. Its failures are the days the customer spec met the works spec and learned the difference, the long straights where the last few horsepower lived behind a seal, and the flat reality that the combine's kindness has a fixed address. Neither house complains on the record; both file the difference carefully, one in a spreadsheet, one in a grudge.

For seventeen seasons the registered specification has been the VAPOR DNPQ V8, one permanent designation kept strictly separate from the DN in the combine's ledger, every annual development package for both houses filed under the same letters. A customer product earns its name by keeping it; the combine has never seen a reason to change a designation both its customers have built their identities around.
quotes: A VAPOR customer liaison: 'The DNPQ comes with drawings, tolerances and a telephone number. What more does a racing team want? Please do not answer that. The DN is not for sale, and the DNPQ has won races on three continents.'
A Dardan forge man: 'It is honest iron, the DNPQ. No mystery, no locked box, no grey men telling my lads what they may not touch. It gives what it gives, and we sharpen the rest.'

lorry-32-v8.name: LORRY 32 V8
lorry-32-v8.tagline: THE WORKHORSE THAT OUTLIVES THE RACECARS AROUND IT.
lorry-32-v8.naming: The Lorry industrial works builds the commercial engines that move the world's freight, and pattern 32 is its racing V8, numbered in the same ledger as the truck line and treated with the same total lack of ceremony.
lorry-32-v8.character: The LORRY 32 V8 is freight-engineering with a racing number: heavy, torquey and unburstable, slow to rev and utterly uninterested in drama. Its ceiling is low and its floor is concrete; it runs cool, drinks steadily, packages big and asks almost nothing of its keepers. In qualifying trim it is humble, in race trim it is indestructible, and in the wet its lazy, traction-rich torque is a gift to patient hands. Its maintenance burden is the lightest on the grid because it was designed for men who fix things with hammers. It serves four houses and four budgets, May's spring, Linden's roots, Rigel's museum and Zeroforce's floor, and it is the same honest engine in all of them.
history: The Lorry works has hauled the world's freight for generations, and pattern 32 began as the firm's quiet wager that an engine built to pull a load for a decade could pull a race car for a season. The doctrine never changed: an engine that thinks it is still hauling something will never once notice a Grand Prix. The works does not chase the grid's fashions; the grid, eventually, comes around to the works' definition of finished.

Its triumphs are the long, unfashionable record of going: Linden's endless finishes and Moreau's hundred-plus starts, May's blooming straight-line afternoons, Zeroforce's stubborn movement at the very floor, and Rigel's storied nights, though the garage's superstitions about the engine are, the works insists, the garage's own. Its failures are the seasons it was simply out-powered, the qualifying sheets where humility has a number and the number is the last row, and the afternoons the freight V8 watched the works engines vanish up the road and kept pulling anyway. It has never once made the front page, which the works considers the highest praise it permits itself.

For seventeen seasons the registered specification has been the LORRY 32 V8, one permanent designation shared by four houses, every annual development package filed against the same pattern number in the freight ledger. The works does not close a line that still ships, and it does not rename one that still pulls. Pattern 32 endures, and the trucks, and the race cars, keep moving.
quotes: A Lorry works man, wiping his hands: 'People laugh at a truck engine until lap fifty. The 32 does not know it is slow, gentlemen. It only knows it is not finished. Neither, usually, is the race.'

A Zeroforce mechanic: 'It sounds like a dying furnace and it will not die. I have welded the car around it a hundred times and never once touched the 32 itself. Ugly, heavy, slow and immortal. We are lucky to have it, and I will deny saying so.'

sega-sg1000-v8.name: SEGA SG1000 V8
sega-sg1000-v8.tagline: THE TEACHING ENGINE OF THE WHOLE GRID.
sega-sg1000-v8.naming: SEGA is the championship's own world-maker, the name over the door of everything, and the SG1000 is the works' thousand-series V8, numbered for the series and built deliberately mild. It exists so that a rookie's first racing engine teaches rather than kills.
sega-sg1000-v8.character: The SEGA SG1000 V8 is mild, honest and indestructible: underpowered by design, with an easy, even delivery and a wide usable band that forgives a clumsy foot and rewards a brave one. It is cheap, frugal, cool-running and classroom-simple to maintain, and in the wet it is the best first teacher there is, progressive and readable where other engines are merely intimidating. In qualifying trim it is humble, in race trim it is the same, because the works does not believe a learning engine should have moods. Its development ceiling is deliberately low: the lesson is elsewhere, and the engine is content to be the blackboard rather than the answer.
history: The SG1000 was built as the front door's own engine, the works' answer to a simple problem: every champion on the grid had to start somewhere, and where they started ought to be honest. The doctrine is pedagogy with a crankshaft: enough power to teach, never enough to flatter, and reliability so complete that a rookie's only variable is himself. Minarae has been its partner since the beginning, and the partnership has launched more careers than any other combination in the sport.

Its triumphs are the honour roll: the champions and race winners whose first seasons it powered, Miller's canyon-brawling days in Minarae country, and the steady stream of graduates poached up the ladder with an SG1000 education stamped on their craft. Its failures are barely failures at all: the seasons it was out-powered by everything with a budget, the trap speeds it will never post, and the mild embarrassment of being the only engine on the grid nobody has ever bothered to protest. It has never been the engine of a scandal, and in this paddock that is its own kind of fame.

For seventeen seasons the registered specification has been the SEGA SG1000 V8, one permanent designation, every annual development package filed against the same thousand-series line, never a rename, never a suffix. The front door does not change its number while kids are still knocking on it. The thousand series stays open, and the next great name is always the one currently strapping in.
quotes: A SEGA works man: 'We could make it faster. We have been asked. But the SG1000's job is to tell a young driver the truth, and the truth at reduced power is still the truth. The ones who master it leave. That is the product working.'
A Minarae mechanic: 'You can leave the SG1000 with a rookie, a toolbox and a rainstorm and come back to find all three intact. Try that with anything else in this paddock. Go on. We will wait.'

misfire-50-v8.name: MISFIRE 50 V8
misfire-50-v8.tagline: NAMED IN DEFIANCE, RACED IN HOPE.
misfire-50-v8.naming: The Misfire Light Engine Company was founded by two brothers whose first prototype coughed on its debut in front of the whole county; they painted the insult on the next engine and never took it off. The 50 marks their fiftieth experiment, the first one that behaved.
misfire-50-v8.character: The MISFIRE 50 V8 is a darling for ten laps and a diva for the rest: light, sweet-revving and genuinely lovely when the air is cool, with a crisp little band of power it will hold so long as nothing asks it to fight. Its cooling thirst is enormous, its internals fragile, its fueling fussy, and its response to heat, shove or rain is to wilt, first in the temperature gauge and then in the lap times. In qualifying trim on a cool morning it is a jewel; in race trim on a warm afternoon it is a prayer. In the wet it is miserable, its maintenance burden is horticultural, and its development ceiling is the radiator's mercy. On its day, though, its day is worth the whole season.
history: The brothers Misfire, and they kept the name out of pure defiance, built light engines the way other men build kites: as light as physics allowed and as brave as materials permitted. The fiftieth experiment was the first to survive a full season, and the glasshouse house of Orchis, tender, sheltered and beautiful, adopted it as its natural heart. The doctrine is horticulture again: cool air, gentle handling, and no sudden violence.

Its triumphs are the flowering days: the one shimmering season the little V8 carried the pale car to results the paddock still mentions in a lowered voice, and the cool-sky afternoons when Tegner's cloud-reading gave it the conditions it was born for and it ran like a prophecy. Its failures are every other kind of afternoon: the warm-up lap steams, the radiators that sigh in the traffic of a real dogfight, the hot Sundays it faded from darling to passenger by half-distance. It has never been an angry engine. It is a tender one, and the frost comes for the tender ones first.

For seventeen seasons the registered specification has been the MISFIRE 50 V8, one permanent designation, every annual development package filed against the same experiment number, never a rename, never a suffix. The brothers were offered, more than once, the chance to rename the one that behaved something more respectable. They refused. The name is the story, the story is the engine, and the engine, on its day, is still worth everything.
quotes: A Misfire brother, defiant to the last: 'Yes, it is called Misfire. Yes, we know. It behaved for seventeen seasons, which is more than your engine can say, and it never once needed a focus group to pick its name.'

An Orchis mechanic, wiping steam off his goggles: 'Cool morning, clean air, gentle hands, and the 50 will break your heart with how lovely it is. Everything else, it breaks your heart the other way. I garden it. I do not race it. There is a difference.'

pond-v8.name: POND V8
pond-v8.tagline: BUILT FOR BOATS, RACED FOR LAUGHS, FEARED ON SUNDAYS.
pond-v8.naming: Pond Marine and Industrial is a foundry on the grey coast that has cast trawler engines for three generations; the racing V8 is its leisure line, adopted by the carnival team because it was cheap, waterproof and impossible to insult.
pond-v8.character: The POND V8 is a marine engine that wandered into the circus and refused to leave: torquey, lazy and deep-chested, slow to rev and practically impossible to drown. Its electrics are waterproof, its cooling is marine-grade, its fuel tolerance oceanic, and its reliability is nautical, which is to say it assumes the journey is long and the weather will be terrible. In qualifying trim it is unhurried; in race trim it is relentless; in the wet it is born again, trawling the field while the thoroughbreds aquaplane. Its maintenance burden is a spanner and a hose, and its development ceiling is low but tide-steady. It is the only engine on the grid that gets stronger as the weekend gets worse.
history: The Pond foundry cast its first racing engine as a sideline, a leisure line for gentlemen who raced yachts, and the carnival outfit bought one because the price was right and the foundry did not ask what a circus wanted with a boat engine. The doctrine is the sea's own: the weather is coming anyway, so build for the worst of it and enjoy the rest.

Its triumphs are the punchlines that landed: the sandbagged Preliminary Races and the flying Sundays, the rostrum steps pinched from grandees mid-title-fight, and the wet weekends when the old marine V8 trawled through the spray collecting positions like driftwood while the expensive engines steamed on the sidelines. Its failures are the dry Saturdays, the unhurried qualifying sheets, the afternoons the tide simply would not come in and the 777 started from the back with no storm to save it. The foundry takes both with the same shrug: the sea gives, the sea withholds, the sea keeps casting.

For seventeen seasons the registered specification has been the POND V8, one permanent designation for the leisure line, every annual development package filed against the same foundry pattern, never a rename, never a suffix. A foundry does not rename a casting that still pours true. The trawlers keep their engine, the carnival keeps its secret, and the grey coast keeps the moulds.
quotes: A Pond foundryman, sea-grey overalls: 'Gentlemen ask if we will build them a proper racing engine. We did. It is the one that finishes. The fact that it also powers trawlers is not an insult, it is the entire reference.'

The Joke garage, grinning under the grease: 'Rain forecast? Wonderful. The Pond does not know it is slow, it only knows it is wet, and wet is where it lives. We tune it, we polish the mask, and yes, we may have oiled the hairpin. Prove it.'

ram-v12.name: RAM V12
ram-v12.tagline: THE BIG TWELVE THAT NEVER ONCE ASKED TO BE EXCUSED.
ram-v12.naming: RAM Heavy Machinery builds the locomotive and harvester engines of the northern plains, and its racing V12 carries no pattern number at all, because the works has only ever built one racing engine and sees no reason to number a single child.
ram-v12.character: The RAM V12 is geological: huge torque, deep voice, slow to rev and utterly without stress, an engine built like a bridge and operated like a harvest. It is heavy, packages like a barn, and makes its power low and early and forever; it is frugal for its size, cool-running, and so overbuilt that its maintenance can be done with farm tools. In qualifying trim it is dignified, in race trim it is eternal, and in the wet its locomotive torque finds grip where lighter, angrier engines spin. Its development ceiling does not interest it. It serves the two most patient houses on the ladder, Lares and Moon, and it is the same honest mountain in both.
history: The RAM works came to racing the way a mountain comes to a valley: slowly, and without asking permission. Its one racing engine was built to prove that the works' twelve-cylinder line could run a Grand Prix the way it ran the northern harvests, all day, every day, until the work was done. The doctrine has never moved: an engine should be the last thing a team worries about, and ideally the thing it never worries about at all.

Its triumphs are the guardian records: Lares' legendary streaks of finishes, a hearth-red car still circulating after the catering had gone home, Arai's sodden-season harvest of points, and Moon's endless faithful orbits at the back, the big twelve still turning long after flashier engines had broken. Its failures are the trap speeds and the qualifying sheets, the afternoons it was lapped twice before lunch and refused to stop anyway, the flat arithmetic that says a barn-sized engine will not win a sprint. The works reads those afternoons the way it reads weather: noted, and of no consequence to the harvest.

For seventeen seasons the registered specification has been the RAM V12, one engine, one name, both houses, every annual development package filed under the same unnumbered designation. The works does not number its child and does not rename it; it simply builds another year's worth, the same as last year's, which is the whole point. The northern line endures, and so does the twelve.
quotes: A RAM works man, unhurried: 'You wish to know the red line? The engine does not have one. It has a horizon. It will still be running when your race is over, your season is over, and your argument about red lines is over.'

A Lares mechanic: 'Feed it, love it, it finishes. Seventeen seasons I have asked the big twelve for one thing, to still be turning at the flag, and it has never once discussed the matter with me. It just does it.'

yougen-v10.name: YOUGEN V10
yougen-v10.tagline: A GENERATOR WITH A RACING LICENSE.
yougen-v10.naming: Yougen Power Systems manufactures the generator sets that light half the circuits on the calendar, and its racing V10 began as a works experiment to prove the industrial block could take a season of Grand Prix abuse. The name is simply the company, because Yougen does not decorate.
yougen-v10.character: The YOUGEN V10 is industrial honesty: gruff, torquey and simple, with a low rev ceiling, an unstressed mid-range and the steady, even thirst of an engine that thinks it is still powering a floodlight. It is heavy, unsophisticated, cool-running and packaged without romance, and its great virtue is that it runs forever and asks almost nothing. In qualifying trim it is honest, in race trim it is stubborn, and in the wet it is unfussed, the same engine in the rain as in the dry because it never claimed to be anything else. Its maintenance burden is a mechanic's holiday. Its development ceiling is modest, and Yougen meets it every season with the quiet pride of a firm whose experiment keeps refusing to fail.
history: Yougen built its first racing engine to settle an argument inside its own works: whether a generator block, engineered for decades of abuse, could survive a season of Grand Prix racing without being redesigned into something precious. It could. The doctrine is the company's own: no decoration, no drama, no excuses, and if the experiment works, you keep running the experiment.

Its triumphs are the grafter's record: Old Boots' full-distance seasons, points stolen from grandees who overcooked it, a chaos-afternoon podium earned on the day the aristocrats fed the barriers, and seventeen seasons of proving that a working engine belongs on the same grid as the jewellery. Its failures are the Saturdays, the trap speeds, the afternoons the low ceiling told the truth and the blue car gave best to everything with a budget. The experiment has never been glamorous. It has also never been wrong, and Yougen renews its racing license every season with the same signature and the same shrug.

For seventeen seasons the registered specification has been the YOUGEN V10, one permanent designation, every annual development package filed against the same experiment, never a rename, never a suffix. A proof does not need a new name every year; it needs only to keep being proven. The generators still light the circuits, and the engine that shares their name still finishes on them.
quotes: A Yougen works man, arms folded: 'Glamour? No. The engine lights half this paddock and finishes in front of half of it too, some Sundays. We build power that does not stop. Racing is simply the most expensive place we demonstrate it.'
A Feet mechanic: 'It is heavy, it is slow to rev, and it has never once left me walking home. The big engines are racehorses, son. The Yougen is a boot. You would be amazed what a boot outlasts.'

sc3000-f12.name: SC3000 F12
sc3000-f12.tagline: TWELVE FLAT CYLINDERS FROM A TWO-MAN WORKSHOP.
sc3000-f12.naming: SC is Sambena Constructions, the privateer's own badge; the F12 is a flat-twelve, the low, wide layout Sambena chose because a man who services his own engine should be able to lay his hands across the whole top of it. It is not, and has never been, a V.
sc3000-f12.character: The SC3000 F12 is a cranky, loveable mountain engine: low, wide and torquey, cold-blooded and stubborn until it warms, then eager to rev with a broad, flat voice all its own. The oiling has its opinions on long corners, the fueling is unfussy, the cooling is mountain-air simple, and every part of it can be reached, freed and refitted by the two hands that built it. In qualifying trim it is game, in race trim it is game, and in the wet it is instinct's friend, predictable at the limit because its builder drives the thing himself. Its maintenance burden is last night's part and a prayer, and its development ceiling is whatever Sambena teaches it next winter.
history: The F12 began as a privateer's answer to a privateer's problem: the works engines were sealed, the customer engines were priced for customers, and a man with a welding torch and a mountain workshop needed twelve cylinders he could reach. So he built his own, flat and low and wide, because a flat engine is an honest engine, everything on top, nothing hidden. The doctrine is the mountain's: build what you can reach, fix what you can carry, and never borrow a part you cannot make.

Its triumphs are the factory embarrassments: the grey number 25 filling LEVEL A mirrors at the hairpin, points banked never bought, and the quiet terror the flat-twelve's broad voice spreads through garages that spend more on hospitality than the whole SC operation spends on racing. Its failures are the shoestring nights: the oiling quirks found the hard way, the parts made at two in the morning and fitted at four, the seasons the budget ran out before the calendar did. It has never been a works engine, and it has never once been beaten for spirit.

For seventeen seasons the registered specification has been the SC3000 F12, one permanent designation, flat twelve always, never a V, never a rename, every annual development package scavenged and filed against the same badge. The engine is the man and the man is the team, and a man does not renumber himself. The mountain pass it was named for is still there, and so is the engine that sang all the way up it.
quotes: Sambena, wiping his hands on a rag: 'Flat twelve, yes, because I can see all of it. Every bolt, every pipe, my own hands on my own engine. The works men lock theirs away in sealed boxes. I would rather reach mine than hide it.'

A rival engineer, grudging: 'It should not work. A home-built flat-twelve in this paddock, in this era. Then you hear it pull out of the hairpin, wide and angry and alive, and you stop saying should and start checking your mirrors.'

corse-v8.name: CORSE V8
corse-v8.tagline: AN OLD SONG, KEPT IN PRODUCTION OUT OF LOVE.
corse-v8.naming: The Corse Foundry is one of the oldest names in the sport's supply trade, fallen on quiet times, and its V8 is the foundry's eternal pattern, cast the same way for a generation because the men who cast it see no reason to stop.
corse-v8.character: The CORSE V8 is an antique kept warm: gentle, worn and charming, with a soft, old-fashioned delivery, modest power and the kind of manners only a very old design can have. It runs cool when loved, grows thirsty when tired, and its electrics are the most fragile thing about it, prone to sulks at the worst possible moment. In qualifying trim it is brave, in race trim it is faithful, and in the wet the old song knows rain, patient and progressive. Its maintenance burden is affection, its development ceiling was reached long ago, and the foundry polishes it still, the way you polish something you have no intention of replacing.
history: The Corse Foundry was casting racing engines before half the current grid was born, and its V8 is less a product than a habit the trade never broke. When the loud new engine houses took the glamour business, the foundry kept its pattern, its price and its dignity, and found its soulmate in Cool, the one team on the ladder that understands an old survivor's worth. The doctrine is continuity: the song is old, the song is good, keep singing.

Its triumphs are the saints' days of the floor: the single points that sent the deckchair garage into raptures, the wet afternoons in Delvaux's home forest when the old V8 idled through the spray and came home with points the works engines drowned trying to take, and the long, stubborn record of simply still being here. Its failures are the coughs to a halt at the worst moments, the electrical sulks, the qualifying sheets from another era. The foundry has buried engine houses younger than its current pattern. It intends to bury a few more.

For seventeen seasons the registered specification has been the CORSE V8, one permanent designation, every annual development package, a gasket here, a casting cleaned there, filed against the same eternal pattern, never a rename, never a suffix. A song you keep in production out of love does not get a new title. The moulds are worn, the pour is true, and the foundry sees no reason to stop.
quotes: A Corse foundryman, among the moulds: 'Everyone asks when we will retire the pattern. We buried three engine companies that asked us that. The V8 stays in production, gentlemen, because it still starts, most mornings, and so do we.'

The Cool garage, feet up: 'She is held together with tape, good humour and whatever the foundry had in the bin, and she still starts. Well. Most mornings. The ones she does not, we have the deckchairs.'

prism-90-v10.name: PRISM 90 V10
prism-90-v10.tagline: THE CLEANEST NOTE IN THE PADDOCK.
prism-90-v10.naming: Prism Precision Works ground the sport's optical instruments before it ground a crankshaft, and 90 is the vee angle of its racing V10, stamped into the name because Prism names things the way it makes them: exactly.
prism-90-v10.character: The PRISM 90 V10 is a jewelled engine: light, crystalline and high-revving, with a clean, expensive note and its best power held in a narrow band at the very top, where only exact hands may visit. It is frugal at full song, cool-running when prepared to the letter, and demanding of preparation measured in millimetres and white gloves. In qualifying trim it is the song itself; in race trim it depends entirely on the gloves. In the wet it is precise rather than brave, its maintenance burden is total and loving, and its reliability is perfect until it is not. Its development ceiling is reached by lapping increments, one exact improvement at a time, because Prism would rather polish one facet for a winter than add ten rough ones.
history: Prism came to engines from optics, and it brought the lens-grinder's faith: that precision is not a means to performance but the thing itself. The racing V10 is built the way a fine lens is ground, no seam allowed to show, no tolerance left vague, and the works' only customer is the jewel house of Iris, which is not a coincidence so much as a marriage of temperaments. The doctrine has never changed: exactness is identity.

Its triumphs are the pole-day songs, the fast-sweeper masterclasses, the drivers' title carried home in violet, and the wet afternoons when Salgado danced the 90 through the spray on the exact edge of its narrow band and made precision look like bravery. Its failures are exact ones: the seasons raw power beat finesse, the races it qualified beautifully and finished fourth, the rare, surgical failures that arrive without vibration, without smoke, without any warning at all. Prism logs those too, in the same hand, because an exact failure is still data.

For seventeen seasons the registered specification has been the PRISM 90 V10, one permanent designation, every annual development package ground and filed against the same figure, never a rename, never a suffix. The angle never changes, the name never changes; a lens is not renamed between polishings. The song stays clean, the band stays narrow, and the works keeps grinding.
quotes: A Prism works man, white gloves: 'People ask for more power. We give them more precision, and the power follows, as it has for seventeen seasons. The 90 is not powerful in the way a hammer is powerful. It is powerful the way a measurement is.'

An Iris mechanic: 'You do not warm up the 90, you calibrate it. Ninety degrees, exactly, every time, or it will not sing. When it sings, gentlemen, there is nothing on this grid I would trade it for.'

bloom-88-v8.name: BLOOM 88 V8
bloom-88-v8.tagline: IT OPENS AT FULL SONG, AND NOT BEFORE.
bloom-88-v8.naming: The Bloom Engine Works is the boutique shop the glasshouse magnate endowed so his flowers would never beat with a borrowed heart. 88 is the pattern the founder chose for balance, two eights mirrored, the way he pruned his trees.
bloom-88-v8.character: The BLOOM 88 V8 builds its power like a held breath: soft and patient at the bottom, then opening through the middle into a fierce, full-song top end that arrives exactly when the afternoon matters most. It is kind in its delivery, pampered in its internals, cooled to glasshouse perfection and measured in its thirst, and it is unbothered in the wet, where its progressive opening is a gentle driver's ally. In qualifying trim it is patient; in race trim, on the grand Sundays, it is the opening itself. Its maintenance burden is horticultural, and its development ceiling is reached only on the big stages, which the works insists is the point of a ceiling.
history: The Bloom works was endowed for one purpose: no flower of the glasshouse would ever race with a borrowed heart. The boutique shop built its V8 the way the gardeners raise the blooms, sheltered, measured, patient, and tuned to open on command rather than on schedule. The doctrine is cultivation: an engine, like an orchid, reveals itself when the conditions are worth it, and not before.

Its triumphs are the grand nights: the floodlit finales and title-deciding Sundays when the 88 opened at full song and the frost-and-magenta car towered over machinery that had spent all season louder. Its failures are the long springs it spent closed, the small Sundays surrendered while it waited, the seasons the arithmetic froze over before the bloom came. The works does not apologize for either. A flower that opened every week would be a weed, and the glasshouse does not raise weeds.

For seventeen seasons the registered specification has been the BLOOM 88 V8, one permanent designation, every annual development package filed against the same mirrored pattern, never a rename, never a suffix. Two eights, the founder said, balanced like a well-pruned tree, and the works has never improved on the argument. The engine closes, the engine waits, and under the same name, the engine opens again.
quotes: A Bloom works man, watering can in hand: 'You cannot rush the 88 and you cannot fool it. It opens when the afternoon is worth opening for. The other engines shout all season; ours speaks seventeen times a year, and the paddock goes quiet to listen.'
An Azalea mechanic: 'Treat it like a machine and it sulks. Treat it like the bloom it is, feed it, shelter it, wait for it, and on the grand Sundays it will hand you the whole season in one lap.'
